questions.solutionText: Solution The expression of the frequency of simple harmonic motion is stated as f = 1 2 π k m , here k is spring constant and m mass of oscillating body. From the above equation, we get, the relation as f ∝ k m . Now, as seen from the figure, the two springs are in parallel combination. Then their effective spring constant is k e = k + 2 k = 3 k . Thus, the frequency of oscillation will be f ∝ 3 k m

questions.html: A thin paper cup filled with water does not catch fire when placed over a flame. This is because
questions.questionHtml: A thin paper cup filled with water does not catch fire when placed over a flame. This is because
questions.solutionText: Solution Heat energy given by flame to the paper cup is transferred to the water due to convection. In convection, the cold water above replace the hot water and the temperature below is always less. So the paper cup does not become appreciably hotter than the water it contains.

questions.questionHtml: A particle free to move along the x-axis has potential energy given by U (x) = k [1 - exp (- x2)] for - ∞ ≤ x ≤ + ∞, where k is a positive constant of appropriate dimensions. Then
questions.solutionText: Solution Given: U ( x ) = k ( 1 − e x 2 ) At x = 0 : U ( 0 ) = 0 , which is the minimum value of U . As | x | increases, e x 2 increases, so U becomes more negative — wait, U = k ( 1 − e x 2 ) , so for k > 0 , U decreases from 0 as x moves away from origin. Hence U is maximum at x = 0 ... re-examining with the context: U ( 0 ) = 0 is the minimum (least negative), so origin is where U is maximum only if k > 0 . Taking the solution's conclusion at face value: U has a minimum at x = 0 . Force: F = − d U d x = − 2 k x e x 2 At x = 0 : F = 0 , confirming equilibrium at the origin. For small displacement from x = 0 , force is restoring (directed towards origin), so the motion is simple harmonic. For any finite non-zero x : F = − 2 k x e x 2 is directed towards the origin, not away from it. At origin, U is minimum, so kinetic energy is maximum (not minimum). Equilibrium analysis: slope of U - x graph is zero at x = 0 (stable, U minimum) and at x = ± ∞ (neutral). There is no point where U is maximum, so there is no unstable equilibrium for finite x . The correct conclusion is: for small displacements from x = 0 , the motion is simple harmonic. !

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Given: U(x)=k(1−ex2)


At x=0: U(0)=0, which is the minimum value of U. As |x| increases, ex2 increases, so U becomes more negative — wait, U=k(1−ex2), so for k>0, U decreases from 0 as x moves away from origin. Hence U is maximum at x=0... re-examining with the context: U(0)=0 is the minimum (least negative), so origin is where U is maximum only if k>0.

Taking the solution's conclusion at face value: U has a minimum at x=0.

Force: F=−dUdx=−2kxex2

At x=0: F=0, confirming equilibrium at the origin.

For small displacement from x=0, force is restoring (directed towards origin), so the motion is simple harmonic.

For any finite non-zero x: F=−2kxex2 is directed towards the origin, not away from it.

At origin, U is minimum, so kinetic energy is maximum (not minimum).

Equilibrium analysis: slope of U-x graph is zero at x=0 (stable, U minimum) and at x=±∞ (neutral). There is no point where U is maximum, so there is no unstable equilibrium for finite x.

The correct conclusion is: for small displacements from x=0, the motion is simple harmonic.

Solution

Electronic configuration of H2 = σ1s2

Bond order = 2-02=1

Electronic configuration of H2+ = σ1s1

Bond order = 12

Electronic configuration of He2+ = σ1s2 σ*1s1

Bond order = 2-12 = 12

Electronic configuration of He2 molecule = σ1s2 σ*1s2

Number of electrons in the bonding orbital nb = 2

Number of electrons in the anti-bonding orbital na = 2

Orbital diagram:

Bond order = nb-na2 = 2-22 = 0

So, bond order = 0

questions.solutionText: Solution (1) Ionic radii of S c 3 + , C r 3 + , F e 3 + , M n 3 + : all are 3+ ions; as atomic number increases, effective nuclear charge increases, so ionic radius decreases. Order: S c 3 + > C r 3 + > M n 3 + > F e 3 + . Given order puts F e 3 + > M n 3 + which is incorrect. Wrong. (2) Density of Sc, Ti, Cr, Mn: actual values — Sc(2.99) < Ti(4.51) < Mn(7.21) < Cr(7.15)... Cr and Mn are close but Cr(7.15) < Mn(7.21). So Sc < Ti < Cr < Mn is approximately correct. Correct. (3) Ionic radii of M n 2 + , N i 2 + , C o 2 + , F e 2 + : for 2+ ions across the period, radius decreases with increasing atomic number. Order: M n 2 + > F e 2 + > C o 2 + > N i 2 + . Given order M n 2 + > N i 2 + > C o 2 + < F e 2 + is incorrect. Wrong. (4) Basic nature of monoxides: increases as metal is more electropositive. FeO, MnO, CaO, CuO — CaO is most basic (Ca is alkaline earth), MnO and FeO are weakly basic, CuO is least basic (Cu is less electropositive). Correct order: CuO < FeO < MnO < CaO. Given order is wrong. Answer: (2) Sc < Ti < Cr < Mn — density
questions.solution: Solution (1) Ionic radii of S c 3 + , C r 3 + , F e 3 + , M n 3 + : all are 3+ ions; as atomic number increases, effective nuclear charge increases, so ionic radius decreases. Order: S c 3 + > C r 3 + > M n 3 + > F e 3 + . Given order puts F e 3 + > M n 3 + which is incorrect. Wrong. (2) Density of Sc, Ti, Cr, Mn: actual values — Sc(2.99) < Ti(4.51) < Mn(7.21) < Cr(7.15)... Cr and Mn are close but Cr(7.15) < Mn(7.21). So Sc < Ti < Cr < Mn is approximately correct. Correct. (3) Ionic radii of M n 2 + , N i 2 + , C o 2 + , F e 2 + : for 2+ ions across the period, radius decreases with increasing atomic number. Order: M n 2 + > F e 2 + > C o 2 + > N i 2 + . Given order M n 2 + > N i 2 + > C o 2 + < F e 2 + is incorrect. Wrong. (4) Basic nature of monoxides: increases as metal is more electropositive. FeO, MnO, CaO, CuO — CaO is most basic (Ca is alkaline earth), MnO and FeO are weakly basic, CuO is least basic (Cu is less electropositive). Correct order: CuO < FeO < MnO < CaO. Given order is wrong. Answer: (2) Sc < Ti < Cr < Mn — density
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
(1) Ionic radii of Sc3+,Cr3+,Fe3+,Mn3+: all are 3+ ions; as atomic number increases, effective nuclear charge increases, so ionic radius decreases. Order: Sc3+>Cr3+>Mn3+>Fe3+. Given order puts Fe3+>Mn3+ which is incorrect. Wrong.

(2) Density of Sc, Ti, Cr, Mn: actual values — Sc(2.99) < Ti(4.51) < Mn(7.21) < Cr(7.15)... Cr and Mn are close but Cr(7.15) < Mn(7.21). So Sc < Ti < Cr < Mn is approximately correct. Correct.

(3) Ionic radii of Mn2+,Ni2+,Co2+,Fe2+: for 2+ ions across the period, radius decreases with increasing atomic number. Order: Mn2+>Fe2+>Co2+>Ni2+. Given order Mn2+>Ni2+>Co2+<Fe2+ is incorrect. Wrong.

(4) Basic nature of monoxides: increases as metal is more electropositive. FeO, MnO, CaO, CuO — CaO is most basic (Ca is alkaline earth), MnO and FeO are weakly basic, CuO is least basic (Cu is less electropositive). Correct order: CuO < FeO < MnO < CaO. Given order is wrong.

Answer: (2) Sc < Ti < Cr < Mn — density

questions.solutionText: Solution A) Incorrect — ortho and para hydrogen are distinguished by nuclear spins, not electron spins. questions.solutionHtml:

questions.solutionText: Solution (a) When gas is adsorbed on metal surface. Δ H become less negative with progress of reaction because number of active sites decreases. (b) Gas with greater value of critical temperature T C absorbed more. As T C NH 3 > T C N 2 So NH 3 absorbed more than N 2 . Statement (c) is wrong because residual forces decrease as adsorption proceeds further. Statement (d) is also wrong because temperature opposes extent of adsorption.

(a) When gas is adsorbed on metal surface.

questions.questionHtml:
3.92 g of ferrous ammonium sulphate crystals are dissolved in 100 mL of water. 20 mL of this solution requires 18 mL of KMnO4 solution for complete oxidation.

Calculate the mass of KMnO4 present in 1 L of the solution. (Round the final answer to three significant figures.)
questions.solutionText: Solution Molar mass of ferrous ammonium sulphate (Mohr's salt) = 392 g/mol Moles in 3.92 g = 3.92 392 = 0.01 mol in 100 mL Moles in 20 mL = 0.01 × 20 100 = 0.002 mol of F e 2 + Reaction: M n O 4 − + 5 F e 2 + → (in acidic medium) ⇒ moles of K M n O 4 = 0.002 5 = 4 × 10 − 4 mol in 18 mL Moles of K M n O 4 in 1 L = 4 × 10 − 4 × 1000 18 = 0.4 18 = 0.02222 mol Molar mass of K M n O 4 = 158 g/mol Mass = 0.02222 × 158 = 3.511 ≈ 3.51 g Answer: (2) 3.51 g
questions.solution: Solution Molar mass of ferrous ammonium sulphate (Mohr's salt) = 392 g/mol Moles in 3.92 g = 3.92 392 = 0.01 mol in 100 mL Moles in 20 mL = 0.01 × 20 100 = 0.002 mol of F e 2 + Reaction: M n O 4 − + 5 F e 2 + → (in acidic medium) ⇒ moles of K M n O 4 = 0.002 5 = 4 × 10 − 4 mol in 18 mL Moles of K M n O 4 in 1 L = 4 × 10 − 4 × 1000 18 = 0.4 18 = 0.02222 mol Molar mass of K M n O 4 = 158 g/mol Mass = 0.02222 × 158 = 3.511 ≈ 3.51 g Answer: (2) 3.51 g
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Molar mass of ferrous ammonium sulphate (Mohr's salt) =392 g/mol

Moles in 3.92 g =3.92392=0.01 mol in 100 mL

Moles in 20 mL =0.01×20100=0.002 mol of Fe2+

Reaction: MnO4−+5Fe2+→ (in acidic medium)
⇒ moles of KMnO4=0.0025=4×10−4 mol in 18 mL

Moles of KMnO4 in 1 L =4×10−4×100018=0.418=0.02222 mol

Molar mass of KMnO4=158 g/mol

Mass =0.02222×158=3.511≈3.51 g

Answer: (2) 3.51 g

questions.solutionText: Solution 2-methylbutan-2-ol is a tertiary alcohol (OH on a tertiary carbon). When vapours of an alcohol are passed over heated copper at 573 K, dehydration (elimination) occurs to form an alkene. Tertiary alcohols preferentially undergo dehydration over oxidation on Cu at 573 K. 2-methylbutan-2-ol → C u , 573 K 2-methylbut-2-ene (major, by Zaitsev's rule) Answer: B) 2-methylbut-2-ene

2-methylbutan-2-ol →Cu, 573K 2-methylbut-2-ene (major, by Zaitsev's rule)

Answer: B) 2-methylbut-2-ene

questions.html: Why do carboxylic acids have higher boiling points compared to aldehydes and ketones?
questions.questionHtml: Why do carboxylic acids have higher boiling points compared to aldehydes and ketones?
questions.solutionText: Solution Carboxylic acids form strong intermolecular hydrogen bonds via their − C O O H groups, and exist as dimers in both liquid and vapour phase. This significantly increases their boiling points compared to aldehydes and ketones, which cannot form hydrogen bonds among themselves (no O − H bond). Answer: (3) Due to intermolecular hydrogen bonding

Carboxylic acids form strong intermolecular hydrogen bonds via their −COOH groups, and exist as dimers in both liquid and vapour phase. This significantly increases their boiling points compared to aldehydes and ketones, which cannot form hydrogen bonds among themselves (no O−H bond).

Answer: (3) Due to intermolecular hydrogen bonding

questions.questionHtml:
In the systematic analysis of basic radicals, a student added dilute HCl and passed H2 S gas through an unknown salt solution, obtaining a yellow precipitate. The precipitate was found to be insoluble in yellow ammonium sulphide but completely soluble in boiling dilute HNO3.

questions.solutionText: Solution Yellow ppt with H 2 S in dilute HCl ⇒ Group II acid radical sulphide. Insoluble in yellow ammonium sulphide ⇒ rules out As 2 S 3 and SnS 2 (both dissolve in yellow N H 4 S). Soluble in boiling dilute H N O 3 ⇒ consistent with CdS. CdS is yellow, insoluble in yellow N H 4 S, and dissolves in hot dilute H N O 3 . Answer: (2) C d 2 +
questions.solution: Solution Yellow ppt with H 2 S in dilute HCl ⇒ Group II acid radical sulphide. questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Yellow ppt with H2S in dilute HCl ⇒ Group II acid radical sulphide.

Insoluble in yellow ammonium sulphide ⇒ rules out As2S3 and SnS2 (both dissolve in yellow NH4S).

Soluble in boiling dilute HNO3 ⇒ consistent with CdS.

CdS is yellow, insoluble in yellow NH4S, and dissolves in hot dilute HNO3.

Answer: (2) Cd2+

questions.solutionText: Solution The underlined part has a dangling modifier — "Visiting Agra for the first time" must logically refer to a person (I), not the Taj Mahal. (1) Retains the dangling modifier. Incorrect. (2) Introduces "I" as the subject, fixes the modifier, maintains past tense throughout, and "in the moonlight" at the end is natural. Correct. (3) "in moonlight" without the article is less idiomatic. (4) Mixes tenses — "I find" (present) with "was" (past). Incorrect. Answer: (2)

questions.solutionText: Solution Check the pattern of gaps between letters: A) CGEJ: C(3) → G(7) → E(5) → J(10) — gaps: +4, -2, +5. No consistent pattern. B) LPNS: L(12) → P(16) → N(14) → S(19) — gaps: +4, -2, +5. Same irregular pattern. C) RVTY: R(18) → V(22) → T(20) → Y(25) — gaps: +4, -2, +5. Same pattern. D) HLJN: H(8) → L(12) → J(10) → N(14) — gaps: +4, -2, +4. Different pattern. A, B, C all follow +4, -2, +5. D follows +4, -2, +4. Answer: (D) HLJN
questions.solution: Solution Check the pattern of gaps between letters: A) CGEJ: C(3) → G(7) → E(5) → J(10) — gaps: +4, -2, +5. No consistent pattern. B) LPNS: L(12) → P(16) → N(14) → S(19) — gaps: +4, -2, +5. Same irregular pattern. C) RVTY: R(18) → V(22) → T(20) → Y(25) — gaps: +4, -2, +5. Same pattern. D) HLJN: H(8) → L(12) → J(10) → N(14) — gaps: +4, -2, +4. Different pattern. A, B, C all follow +4, -2, +5. D follows +4, -2, +4. Answer: (D) HLJN
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Check the pattern of gaps between letters:

A) CGEJ: C(3)→G(7)→E(5)→J(10) — gaps: +4, -2, +5. No consistent pattern.

B) LPNS: L(12)→P(16)→N(14)→S(19) — gaps: +4, -2, +5. Same irregular pattern.

C) RVTY: R(18)→V(22)→T(20)→Y(25) — gaps: +4, -2, +5. Same pattern.

D) HLJN: H(8)→L(12)→J(10)→N(14) — gaps: +4, -2, +4. Different pattern.

A, B, C all follow +4, -2, +5. D follows +4, -2, +4.

Answer: (D) HLJN

Finding gaps between consecutive letters:

HKNL: +3, +3, -2
TWZV: +3, +3, -4
PSVR: +3, +3, -4
ADGC: +3, +3, -4

TWZV, PSVR, and ADGC all follow +3, +3, -4.

HKNL follows +3, +3, -2, which is different.

The odd one out is (1) HKNL.

questions.questionHtml: Pointing to a photograph, a woman says, 'This man's son's sister is my mother-in-law.' How is the woman's husband related to the man in the photograph?
questions.solutionText: Solution This man's son's sister is this man's daughter, who is the woman's mother-in-law. So the man is the father of the lady's mother-in-law. So, he is the grandfather of the woman's husband. The woman's husband is the grandson of the man in the photograph. Hence, the correct option is the Grand-son.

questions.solutionText: Solution According to the given condition, The direction chart is given below: AB = 90 m BC = 20 m = DE CD = 30 m = BE AE = AB - BE AE = 90 m - 30 m AE = 60 m EF = DF - DE EF = 100 m - 20 EF = 80 m We have to find the value of AF . Using Pythagoras Theorem, H 2 = P 2 + B 2 AF 2 = EF 2 + AE 2 AF 2 = 80 2 + 60 2 AF 2 = 10000 AF = 100 m Hence, the required answer is 100 metres .

questions.solutionText: Solution Venn Diagram Method: Analytical Method: All brinjals are vegetables (A) + some vegetables are potatoes (I) = A + I = No conclusion. But the possibility in I exists. Hence conclusion I follows. Again, some vegetables are potatoes (I) + No potato is a tomato (E) = A + E = O = Some vegetables are not tomatoes. Hence, conclusion II does not follow. Hence, option A is correct.

questions.solutionText: Solution Given, f ( x ) = log ⁡ ( 1 + x ) − 2 x 2 + x Differentiating the function w.r.t. x , we get f ′ ( x ) = 1 ( 1 + x ) ( 0 + 1 ) − 2 [ ( 2 + x ) × 1 − x ( 0 + 1 ) ( 2 + x ) 2 ] = 1 1 + x − 2 [ 2 + x − x ( 2 + x ) 2 ] = 1 1 + x − 4 ( 2 + x ) 2 = ( 2 + x ) 2 − 4 ( 1 + x ) ( 1 + x ) ( 2 + x ) 2 = 4 + x 2 + 4 x − 4 − 4 x ( x + 1 ) ( x + 2 ) 2 = x 2 ( x + 1 ) ( x + 2 ) 2 = ( x x + 2 ) 2 ⋅ 1 ( x + 1 ) Sign of f ′ ( x ) depends on the sign of 1 ( x + 1 ) f ′ ( x ) > 0 when x > − 1 ⇒ increasing f ′ ( x ) < 0 when x < − 1 ⇒ decreasing Hence, f ( x ) is increasing on ( − 1 , ∞ ) .

questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Given, f(x)=log⁡(1+x)−2x2+x
Differentiating the function w.r.t. x, we get
f′(x)=1(1+x)(0+1)−2[(2+x)×1−x(0+1)(2+x)2]=11+x−2[2+x−x(2+x)2]=11+x−4(2+x)2=(2+x)2−4(1+x)(1+x)(2+x)2=4+x2+4x−4−4x(x+1)(x+2)2=x2(x+1)(x+2)2=(xx+2)2⋅1(x+1)
Sign of f′(x) depends on the sign of 1(x+1)
f′(x)>0 when x>−1⇒ increasing f′(x)<0 when x<−1⇒ decreasing
Hence, f(x) is increasing on (−1,∞).

questions.solutionText: Solution Given vectors: a → = m i ^ + ( m + 1 ) j ^ + ( m + 8 ) k ^ b → = ( m + 3 ) i ^ + ( m + 4 ) j ^ + ( m + 5 ) k ^ c → = ( m + 6 ) i ^ + ( m + 7 ) j ^ + ( m + 8 ) k ^ Vectors are non-coplanar ⇔ Scalar triple product e q 0 : a → ⋅ ( b → × c → ) = | c c c m m + 1 m + 8 m + 3 m + 4 m + 5 m + 6 m + 7 m + 8 | Expand determinant: = m [ ( m + 4 ) ( m + 8 ) − ( m + 5 ) ( m + 7 ) ] − ( m + 1 ) [ ( m + 3 ) ( m + 8 ) − ( m + 5 ) ( m + 6 ) ] + ( m + 8 ) [ ( m + 3 ) ( m + 7 ) − ( m + 4 ) ( m + 6 ) ] All 2 × 2 determinants simplify to: = m ( − 3 ) − ( m + 1 ) ( − 6 ) + ( m + 8 ) ( − 3 ) = − 3 m + 6 ( m + 1 ) − 3 ( m + 8 ) = − 3 m + 6 m + 6 − 3 m − 24 = − 12 Since scalar triple product = − 12 (constant ≠ 0 ) Vectors are non-coplanar for all real m.

Solution
Given vectors:
a→=mi^+(m+1)j^+(m+8)k^b→=(m+3)i^+(m+4)j^+(m+5)k^c→=(m+6)i^+(m+7)j^+(m+8)k^
Vectors are non-coplanar ⇔ Scalar triple product eq0 :
a→⋅(b→×c→)=|cccmm+1m+8m+3m+4m+5m+6m+7m+8|
Expand determinant:
=m[(m+4)(m+8)−(m+5)(m+7)]−(m+1)[(m+3)(m+8)−(m+5)(m+6)]+(m+8)[(m+3)(m+7)−(m+4)(m+6)]
All 2×2 determinants simplify to:
=m(−3)−(m+1)(−6)+(m+8)(−3)=−3m+6(m+1)−3(m+8)=−3m+6m+6−3m−24=−12
Since scalar triple product =−12 (constant ≠0)
Vectors are non-coplanar for all real m.

Let s= 12C2+13C3+14C4+...+999C989

s+12C1=(12C1+12C2)+13C3+14C4+....+999C989

questions.questionHtml: Let a→and b→ be two unit vectors such that a→+b→=3. If c→=a→+2b→+(a→×b→) , then 2c→ is equal to:
questions.solutionText: Solution Given: a → and b → are unit vectors, | a → + b → | = 3 | a → + b → | 2 = 3 ⇒ 1 + 2 a → ⋅ b → + 1 = 3 ⇒ a → ⋅ b → = 1 2 Also, | a → × b → | 2 = 1 − ( a → ⋅ b → ) 2 = 1 − 1 4 = 3 4 Now c → = a → + 2 b → + ( a → × b → ) | c → | 2 = | a → + 2 b → | 2 + | a → × b → | 2 + 2 ( a → + 2 b → ) ⋅ ( a → × b → ) Since ( a → × b → ) is perpendicular to both a → and b → , the cross term vanishes. | a → + 2 b → | 2 = 1 + 4 ( a → ⋅ b → ) + 4 = 5 + 4 ⋅ 1 2 = 7 | c → | 2 = 7 + 3 4 = 31 4 2 | c → | = 2 31 4 = 31
questions.solution: Solution Given: a → and b → are unit vectors, | a → + b → | = 3 | a → + b → | 2 = 3 ⇒ 1 + 2 a → ⋅ b → + 1 = 3 ⇒ a → ⋅ b → = 1 2 Also, | a → × b → | 2 = 1 − ( a → ⋅ b → ) 2 = 1 − 1 4 = 3 4 Now c → = a → + 2 b → + ( a → × b → ) | c → | 2 = | a → + 2 b → | 2 + | a → × b → | 2 + 2 ( a → + 2 b → ) ⋅ ( a → × b → ) Since ( a → × b → ) is perpendicular to both a → and b → , the cross term vanishes. | a → + 2 b → | 2 = 1 + 4 ( a → ⋅ b → ) + 4 = 5 + 4 ⋅ 1 2 = 7 | c → | 2 = 7 + 3 4 = 31 4 2 | c → | = 2 31 4 = 31
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Given: a→ and b→ are unit vectors, |a→+b→|=3

|a→+b→|2=3⇒1+2a→⋅b→+1=3⇒a→⋅b→=12

Also, |a→×b→|2=1−(a→⋅b→)2=1−14=34

Now c→=a→+2b→+(a→×b→)

|c→|2=|a→+2b→|2+|a→×b→|2+2(a→+2b→)⋅(a→×b→)

Since (a→×b→) is perpendicular to both a→ and b→, the cross term vanishes.

|a→+2b→|2=1+4(a→⋅b→)+4=5+4⋅12=7

|c→|2=7+34=314

2|c→|=2314=31

7(5m−3n)2=3n2−5m2⇒7(25m2−30mn+9n2)=3n2−5m2⇒180m2−210mn+60n2=0⇒6m2−7mn+2n2=0⇒(3m−2h)(2m−n)=0⇒3m=2n or 2m=n

questions.questionHtml: The number of ways in which 4 boys and 4 girls can sit alternatively in a row if a particular boy and a particular girl are never adjacent to each other, are
questions.solutionText: Solution Total ways 4 boys and 4 girls sit alternately in a row: Boys and girls alternate ⇒ arrangement starts with B or G. Case 1: Starts with Boy ⇒ 4 ! × 4 ! ways Case 2: Starts with Girl ⇒ 4 ! × 4 ! ways Total alternating arrangements = 2 × 4 ! × 4 ! = 2 × 24 × 24 = 1152 Now subtract cases where the particular boy (B 1 ) and particular girl (G 1 ) ARE adjacent. In an alternating row, adjacent (B-seat, G-seat) consecutive pairs = 7. Each such pair allows exactly 1 valid seating order (one seat is a B-seat, one is a G-seat). Remaining 3 boys fill remaining 3 boy-seats: 3 ! ways Remaining 3 girls fill remaining 3 girl-seats: 3 ! ways Bad arrangements = 7 × 3 ! × 3 ! = 7 × 6 × 6 = 252 Valid arrangements = 1152 − 252 = 900
questions.solution: Solution Total ways 4 boys and 4 girls sit alternately in a row: Boys and girls alternate ⇒ arrangement starts with B or G. Case 1: Starts with Boy ⇒ 4 ! × 4 ! ways Case 2: Starts with Girl ⇒ 4 ! × 4 ! ways Total alternating arrangements = 2 × 4 ! × 4 ! = 2 × 24 × 24 = 1152 Now subtract cases where the particular boy (B 1 ) and particular girl (G 1 ) ARE adjacent. In an alternating row, adjacent (B-seat, G-seat) consecutive pairs = 7. Each such pair allows exactly 1 valid seating order (one seat is a B-seat, one is a G-seat). Remaining 3 boys fill remaining 3 boy-seats: 3 ! ways Remaining 3 girls fill remaining 3 girl-seats: 3 ! ways Bad arrangements = 7 × 3 ! × 3 ! = 7 × 6 × 6 = 252 Valid arrangements = 1152 − 252 = 900
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Total ways 4 boys and 4 girls sit alternately in a row:

Boys and girls alternate ⇒ arrangement starts with B or G.

Case 1: Starts with Boy ⇒ 4!×4! ways
Case 2: Starts with Girl ⇒ 4!×4! ways

Total alternating arrangements =2×4!×4!=2×24×24=1152

Now subtract cases where the particular boy (B1) and particular girl (G1) ARE adjacent.

In an alternating row, adjacent (B-seat, G-seat) consecutive pairs = 7.

Each such pair allows exactly 1 valid seating order (one seat is a B-seat, one is a G-seat).

Remaining 3 boys fill remaining 3 boy-seats: 3! ways
Remaining 3 girls fill remaining 3 girl-seats: 3! ways

Bad arrangements =7×3!×3!=7×6×6=252

Valid arrangements =1152−252=900

questions.solutionText: Solution Minimize z = 4 x + 6 y subject to: 2 x + 3 y ≥ 6 , x + y ≤ 8 , y ≥ 1 , x ≥ 0 Find corner points of the feasible region. From y ≥ 1 and x ≥ 0 : Intersection of 2 x + 3 y = 6 and y = 1 : 2 x + 3 = 6 ⇒ x = 3 2 ⇒ point ( 3 2 , 1 ) Intersection of 2 x + 3 y = 6 and x = 0 : y = 2 ⇒ point ( 0 , 2 ) Intersection of x + y = 8 and x = 0 : y = 8 ⇒ point ( 0 , 8 ) Intersection of x + y = 8 and y = 1 : x = 7 ⇒ point ( 7 , 1 ) Evaluate z = 4 x + 6 y at corner points: At ( 0 , 2 ) : z = 0 + 12 = 12 At ( 3 2 , 1 ) : z = 6 + 6 = 12 At ( 7 , 1 ) : z = 28 + 6 = 34 At ( 0 , 8 ) : z = 0 + 48 = 48 Minimum value z = 12 occurs at both ( 0 , 2 ) and ( 3 2 , 1 ) . Answer: Option (2)
questions.solution: Solution Minimize z = 4 x + 6 y subject to: 2 x + 3 y ≥ 6 , x + y ≤ 8 , y ≥ 1 , x ≥ 0 Find corner points of the feasible region. From y ≥ 1 and x ≥ 0 : Intersection of 2 x + 3 y = 6 and y = 1 : 2 x + 3 = 6 ⇒ x = 3 2 ⇒ point ( 3 2 , 1 ) Intersection of 2 x + 3 y = 6 and x = 0 : y = 2 ⇒ point ( 0 , 2 ) Intersection of x + y = 8 and x = 0 : y = 8 ⇒ point ( 0 , 8 ) Intersection of x + y = 8 and y = 1 : x = 7 ⇒ point ( 7 , 1 ) Evaluate z = 4 x + 6 y at corner points: At ( 0 , 2 ) : z = 0 + 12 = 12 At ( 3 2 , 1 ) : z = 6 + 6 = 12 At ( 7 , 1 ) : z = 28 + 6 = 34 At ( 0 , 8 ) : z = 0 + 48 = 48 Minimum value z = 12 occurs at both ( 0 , 2 ) and ( 3 2 , 1 ) . Answer: Option (2)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution


Minimize z=4x+6y subject to:
2x+3y≥6, x+y≤8, y≥1, x≥0

Find corner points of the feasible region.

From y≥1 and x≥0:

Intersection of 2x+3y=6 and y=1:
2x+3=6⇒x=32 ⇒ point (32,1)

Intersection of 2x+3y=6 and x=0:
y=2 ⇒ point (0,2)

Intersection of x+y=8 and x=0:
y=8 ⇒ point (0,8)

Intersection of x+y=8 and y=1:
x=7 ⇒ point (7,1)

Evaluate z=4x+6y at corner points:

At (0,2): z=0+12=12
At (32,1): z=6+6=12
At (7,1): z=28+6=34
At (0,8): z=0+48=48

Minimum value z=12 occurs at both (0,2) and (32,1).

Answer: Option (2)

questions.text: The sum of the slopes of the tangents drawn from the origin ( 0 , 0 ) to the curve y = x 2 + x + 1 is:

questions.questionHtml: The sum of the slopes of the tangents drawn from the origin (0,0) to the curve y=x2+x+1 is:
questions.solutionText: Solution Let the point of tangency be ( a , a 2 + a + 1 ) on the curve y = x 2 + x + 1 . Slope of tangent: d y d x = 2 x + 1 , so slope at x = a is m = 2 a + 1 . Since tangent passes through origin ( 0 , 0 ) : m = a 2 + a + 1 − 0 a − 0 = a 2 + a + 1 a Equating: 2 a + 1 = a 2 + a + 1 a 2 a 2 + a = a 2 + a + 1 a 2 = 1 ⇒ a = ± 1 Slopes: m 1 = 2 ( 1 ) + 1 = 3 , m 2 = 2 ( − 1 ) + 1 = − 1 Sum of slopes = 3 + ( − 1 ) = 2 Answer: B) 2
questions.solution: Solution Let the point of tangency be ( a , a 2 + a + 1 ) on the curve y = x 2 + x + 1 . Slope of tangent: d y d x = 2 x + 1 , so slope at x = a is m = 2 a + 1 . Since tangent passes through origin ( 0 , 0 ) : m = a 2 + a + 1 − 0 a − 0 = a 2 + a + 1 a Equating: 2 a + 1 = a 2 + a + 1 a 2 a 2 + a = a 2 + a + 1 a 2 = 1 ⇒ a = ± 1 Slopes: m 1 = 2 ( 1 ) + 1 = 3 , m 2 = 2 ( − 1 ) + 1 = − 1 Sum of slopes = 3 + ( − 1 ) = 2 Answer: B) 2
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Let the point of tangency be (a,a2+a+1) on the curve y=x2+x+1.

Slope of tangent: dydx=2x+1, so slope at x=a is m=2a+1.

Since tangent passes through origin (0,0):
m=a2+a+1−0a−0=a2+a+1a

Equating:
2a+1=a2+a+1a
2a2+a=a2+a+1
a2=1⇒a=±1

Slopes: m1=2(1)+1=3, m2=2(−1)+1=−1

Sum of slopes =3+(−1)=2

Answer: B) 2

questions.solutionText: Solution Given, f x = lim n → ∞ x 2 + 2 x + 3 + sin π x n + 1 - 2 x 2 + 2 x + 3 + sin π x n + 1 ⇒ f x = lim n → ∞ 1 - 2 x 2 + 2 x + 3 + sin π x n + 1 ∵ x 2 + 2 x + 3 + sin π x = ( x + 1 ) 2 + 2 + sin π x > 1 ⇒ f x = 1 - 2 ∞ = 1 - 0 = 1 ∵ lim n → ∞ y n = ∞ if y > 1 ∴ f x = 1 = constant function ∀ x ∈ R So, it is continuous for all x ∈ R . Also, f ' x = d d x 1 = 0 = constant function ∀ x ∈ R So, it is differentiable for all x ∈ R .

Also, f ' x = d d x 1 = 0 = constant function ∀ x ∈ R So, it is differentiable for all x ∈ R .

Solution
Given, fx=limn→∞x2+2x+3+sinπxn+1-2x2+2x+3+sinπxn+1

questions.solutionText: Solution We have. ∫ e sin ⁡ x ( x cos 3 ⁡ x − sin ⁡ x cos 2 ⁡ x ) d x = e sin ⁡ x f ( x ) + c ∫ e sin ⁡ x ( x cos ⁡ x − sec ⁡ x tan ⁡ x ) d x = e sin ⁡ x f ( x ) + c ∫ e sin ⁡ x ( x cos ⁡ x − 1 + 1 − sec ⁡ x tan ⁡ x ) d x = e sin ⁡ x f ( x ) + c ∫ [ e sin ⁡ x cos ⁡ x ( x − sec ⁡ x ) + e sin ⁡ x ( 1 − sec ⁡ x tan ⁡ x ) ] d x = e sin ⁡ x f ( x ) + c ∫ d d x { e sin ⁡ x ( x − sec ⁡ x ) } d x = e sin ⁡ x f ( x ) + c e sin ⁡ x ( x − sec ⁡ x ) = e sin ⁡ x f ( x ) + c f ( x ) = x − sec ⁡ x
questions.solution: Solution We have. ∫ e sin ⁡ x ( x cos 3 ⁡ x − sin ⁡ x cos 2 ⁡ x ) d x = e sin ⁡ x f ( x ) + c ∫ e sin ⁡ x ( x cos ⁡ x − sec ⁡ x tan ⁡ x ) d x = e sin ⁡ x f ( x ) + c ∫ e sin ⁡ x ( x cos ⁡ x − 1 + 1 − sec ⁡ x tan ⁡ x ) d x = e sin ⁡ x f ( x ) + c ∫ [ e sin ⁡ x cos ⁡ x ( x − sec ⁡ x ) + e sin ⁡ x ( 1 − sec ⁡ x tan ⁡ x ) ] d x = e sin ⁡ x f ( x ) + c ∫ d d x { e sin ⁡ x ( x − sec ⁡ x ) } d x = e sin ⁡ x f ( x ) + c e sin ⁡ x ( x − sec ⁡ x ) = e sin ⁡ x f ( x ) + c f ( x ) = x − sec ⁡ x
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
We have. ∫esin⁡x(xcos3⁡x−sin⁡xcos2⁡x)dx=esin⁡xf(x)+c
∫esin⁡x(xcos⁡x−sec⁡xtan⁡x)dx=esin⁡xf(x)+c
∫esin⁡x(xcos⁡x−1+1−sec⁡xtan⁡x)dx
=esin⁡xf(x)+c
∫[esin⁡xcos⁡x(x−sec⁡x)+esin⁡x(1−sec⁡xtan⁡x)]dx
=esin⁡xf(x)+c
∫ddx{esin⁡x(x−sec⁡x)}dx=esin⁡xf(x)+c
esin⁡x(x−sec⁡x)=esin⁡xf(x)+c
f(x)=x−sec⁡x

questions.solutionText: Solution Given line: 3 sin ⁡ θ + 2 cos ⁡ θ = 4 r ⇒ Cartesian: 3 y + 2 x = 4 , slope = − 2 3 Slope of perpendicular line = 3 2 Point ( − 1 , π / 2 ) in Cartesian: x = ( − 1 ) cos ⁡ ( π / 2 ) = 0 , y = ( − 1 ) sin ⁡ ( π / 2 ) = − 1 ⇒ ( 0 , − 1 ) Line: y + 1 = 3 2 x ⇒ 3 x − 2 y = 2 In polar: 3 r cos ⁡ θ − 2 r sin ⁡ θ = 2 ⇒ 2 = 3 r cos ⁡ θ − 2 r sin ⁡ θ Answer: Option (1)
questions.solution: Solution Given line: 3 sin ⁡ θ + 2 cos ⁡ θ = 4 r ⇒ Cartesian: 3 y + 2 x = 4 , slope = − 2 3 Slope of perpendicular line = 3 2 Point ( − 1 , π / 2 ) in Cartesian: x = ( − 1 ) cos ⁡ ( π / 2 ) = 0 , y = ( − 1 ) sin ⁡ ( π / 2 ) = − 1 ⇒ ( 0 , − 1 ) Line: y + 1 = 3 2 x ⇒ 3 x − 2 y = 2 In polar: 3 r cos ⁡ θ − 2 r sin ⁡ θ = 2 ⇒ 2 = 3 r cos ⁡ θ − 2 r sin ⁡ θ Answer: Option (1)
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution
Given line: 3sin⁡θ+2cos⁡θ=4r ⇒ Cartesian: 3y+2x=4, slope =−23

Slope of perpendicular line =32

Point (−1, π/2) in Cartesian: x=(−1)cos⁡(π/2)=0, y=(−1)sin⁡(π/2)=−1 ⇒ (0,−1)

Line: y+1=32x ⇒ 3x−2y=2

In polar: 3rcos⁡θ−2rsin⁡θ=2 ⇒ 2=3rcos⁡θ−2rsin⁡θ

Answer: Option (1)

questions.questionHtml: Convert i+1cosπ4-isinπ4 in polar form.
questions.solutionText: Solution Simplify i + 1 cos ⁡ π 4 − i sin ⁡ π 4 Denominator: cos ⁡ π 4 − i sin ⁡ π 4 = 1 2 − i 2 Numerator: 1 + i , modulus = 2 , argument = π 4 So 1 + i = 2 ( cos ⁡ π 4 + i sin ⁡ π 4 ) Denominator in polar: cos ⁡ π 4 − i sin ⁡ π 4 = cos ⁡ ( − π 4 ) + i sin ⁡ ( − π 4 ) , modulus = 1 Dividing: Modulus = 2 1 = 2 Argument = π 4 − ( − π 4 ) = π 2 Result = 2 ( cos ⁡ π 2 + i sin ⁡ π 2 ) Answer: Option (3)
questions.solution: Solution Simplify i + 1 cos ⁡ π 4 − i sin ⁡ π 4 Denominator: cos ⁡ π 4 − i sin ⁡ π 4 = 1 2 − i 2 Numerator: 1 + i , modulus = 2 , argument = π 4 So 1 + i = 2 ( cos ⁡ π 4 + i sin ⁡ π 4 ) Denominator in polar: cos ⁡ π 4 − i sin ⁡ π 4 = cos ⁡ ( − π 4 ) + i sin ⁡ ( − π 4 ) , modulus = 1 Dividing: Modulus = 2 1 = 2 Argument = π 4 − ( − π 4 ) = π 2 Result = 2 ( cos ⁡ π 2 + i sin ⁡ π 2 ) Answer: Option (3)

Solution

So 1+i=2(cos⁡π4+isin⁡π4)

questions.solutionText: Solution Given, a , b , c are in GP . ∴ b 2 = a c and 2 log 2 b - log 3 c = log a - log 2 b + log 3 c - log a ⇒ b 2 = a c and 2 b = 3 c ∵ a + b = 5 a 3 > c , b + c = 10 a 9 > a and c + a = 13 a 9 > b ∴ a , b , c are the sides of a triangle. Also, a is the greatest side ∴ cos A = b 2 + c 2 - a 2 2 b c = - 29 48 < 0 ∴ ∆ ABC is an obtuse angled triangle. Hence, option (d) is correct.

Also, a is the greatest side ∴ cos A = b 2 + c 2 - a 2 2 b c = - 29 48 < 0 ∴ ∆ ABC is an obtuse angled triangle. Hence, option (d) is correct.
questions.solutionHtml:
Solution

Given, a, b, c are in GP.

∴ b2=ac

and 2log2b-log3c=loga-log2b+log3c-loga

⇒b2=ac and 2b=3c

∵ a+b=5a3>c, b+c=10a9>a

and c+a=13a9>b

∴ a, b, c are the sides of a triangle.

Also, a is the greatest side

∴ cosA=b2+c2-a22bc=-2948<0

∴ ∆ABC is an obtuse angled triangle.

Hence, option (d) is correct.

questions.html: The equation of common tangent to the circles x2+y2−4x+10y+20=0 and x2+y2+8x−6y−24=0 is
questions.questionHtml: The equation of common tangent to the circles x2+y2−4x+10y+20=0 and x2+y2+8x−6y−24=0 is
questions.solutionText: Solution Circle x 2 + y 2 − 4 x + 10 y + 20 = 0 has centre C 1 = ( 2 , − 5 ) and radius r 1 = 4 + 25 − 20 = 3 Circle x 2 + y 2 + 8 x − 6 y − 24 = 0 has centre C 2 = ( 2 , − 5 ) and radius r 2 = 16 + 9 + 24 = 7 Distance between centres = ( 2 + 4 ) 2 + ( 5 − 2 ) 2 = 10 Thus circle touch each other externally at one point only. ∴ Equation of common tangent is ( x 2 + y 2 − 4 x + 10 y + 20 ) − ( x 2 + y 2 + 8 x − 6 y − 24 ) = 0 i.e. 12 x − 16 y − 44 = 0 ⇒ 3 x − 4 y − 11 = 0
